news_summary: New discoveries of ancient fossils have provided groundbreaking insights into early life in the deep ocean and the evolutionary origins of spiders.
news_summary_fr: De nouvelles découvertes de fossiles anciens ont apporté des éclairages révolutionnaires sur les premières formes de vie dans les profondeurs océaniques et sur les origines évolutives des araignées.
news_summary_es: Los nuevos hallazgos de fósiles antiguos han aportado conocimientos revolucionarios sobre la vida primitiva en las profundidades del océano y los orígenes evolutivos de las arañas.

articles.title: Strange “elephant skin” rocks reveal ancient life in the dark ocean
articles.summary: A puzzling wrinkled rock formation in Morocco has led scientists to rethink where ancient microbes could live. Instead of shallow, sunlit waters, these microbes may have thrived deep in the ocean, fueled by chemicals delivered by underwater landslides. The discovery suggests that dark, nutrient-rich environments hosted thriving ecosystems much earlier than expected. It also raises the possibility that many similar fossils have been overlooked or misinterpreted.
articles.content:
In 2016, geologist Rowan Martindale was hiking across a hillside in Morocco when something unusual caught her eye. A slab of sedimentary rock was covered in a wrinkled texture that looked strikingly like elephant skin. The pattern was so unexpected that it immediately stood out.

"I looked at the wrinkles and I was like, 'These aren't supposed to be in rocks like this. What the heck is going on?'" said Martindale, an associate professor at The University of Texas at Austin's Jackson School of Geosciences.

Rock textures can reveal the processes that shaped them over millions of years. To Martindale, the wrinkled surface looked exactly like fossilized microbial mats. These structures form when communities of microbes grow across sediment, leaving behind distinctive patterns. In this case, the textures appeared to preserve a dense layer of microbial life that existed more than 180 million years ago during the Early Jurassic.

Martindale recognized the pattern right away. During graduate school, she had studied similar textures through photos and samples shared by a lab mate who focused on microbial fossils from the Early Triassic.

A Puzzle in the Wrong Place

There was one major issue. The setting did not match what scientists expected.

The rock layer where the wrinkles formed originated in deep ocean water, nearly 600 feet below the surface. However, scientists have long believed that these kinds of microbial wrinkle structures only formed in shallow environments. In those settings, microbes could rely on sunlight for energy and avoid animals that would otherwise consume them, especially during stressful periods or after mass extinctions.

In deeper water, similar patterns are usually explained in a different way. Geologists often attribute them to underwater landslides that push sediment into ridges and grooves. But Martindale was not convinced. The patterns she saw had the clear signature of microbial activity.

"It was one of those things, knowing what to look for and having that 'search image' of wrinkle structures in my head, that made me want to stop and dig into this," she said.

A New Explanation for Deep-Sea Wrinkle Structures

In a recent study published in Geology, Martindale and her colleagues propose a new interpretation that connects geological processes with biological activity. They suggest that while an underwater landslide did occur, it did not directly create the wrinkles. Instead, it delivered nutrients to the seafloor, allowing microbes to grow and form the structures.

According to the team, these microbes did not depend on sunlight. Instead, they likely relied on chemicals for energy, a process known as chemosynthetic. The influx of nutrients from the landslide may have supported these communities, while the release of toxic sulfur compounds could have discouraged other marine life from disturbing them.

Modern Clues From Deep Ocean Ecosystems

Similar ecosystems exist in today's oceans. Some microbial mats thrive in deep, dark environments by feeding on chemical energy rather than sunlight. One example can be found on whale carcasses that sink to the seafloor. These "whale fall" sites create temporary but rich ecosystems where microbes quickly colonize and flourish.

Jake Bailey, a professor at the University of Minnesota who studies how microbes influence Earth's systems, said the findings challenge long-standing assumptions about these rock structures.

"In the present, some of the largest microbial ecosystems on our planet are found in the dark ocean," said Bailey, who was not involved with the research. "The research here shows that certain ancient sedimentary structures may record the presence of these chemolithotrophs rather than phototrophs (organisms that need sunlight to make energy). "

Rethinking Fossils in the Rock Record

Martindale explained that this discovery could have broad implications. If chemosynthetic microbial communities were more widespread than previously believed, their fossils may be more common as well. However, scientists may have overlooked them by interpreting wrinkled rock textures as purely physical formations.

Part of the challenge lies in the language used to describe these features. Without clear terminology, it can be difficult to distinguish between structures formed by physical forces and those created by living organisms.

"The terminology is pretty lax," Martindale said. "Wrinkly can be mean lots of things, so there's a lack of diagnostic language."

Following an Unexpected Scientific Path

Martindale typically studies ancient coral reefs and mass extinctions. She did not expect this observation to lead her into the study of deep-sea microbial mats. But the mystery proved too compelling to ignore.

"It's really cool to have gone in this direction that I totally wasn't expecting," she said. "There was no hypothesis that I would find these microbial mats here. It was just being in the right place at the right time, with the right search image. And then being so stubborn as to not let go of it."

The research was funded by the National Science Foundation.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery that challenges existing assumptions about ancient microbial life, revealing that deep ocean environments hosted thriving ecosystems much earlier than previously thought. This breakthrough has broad implications for paleontology and our understanding of early life on Earth, representing a meaningful advancement in scientific knowledge with potential wide-reaching impact.
articles.personality_title: Wrinkled rocks in Morocco reveal ancient deep-sea life
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists have long believed that certain wrinkled patterns in ancient rocks were formed only in shallow, sunlit waters, where microbes could grow using sunlight. These patterns were thought to be rare or absent in deep ocean environments.

**What happened** – In 2016, geologist Rowan Martindale found unusual wrinkled rock textures in Morocco that looked like fossilized microbial mats. These rocks formed nearly 600 feet below the ocean surface more than 180 million years ago. Martindale and her team studied these textures and discovered that instead of sunlight, microbes in the deep ocean likely used chemicals from underwater landslides to survive and create these patterns.

**Impact** – This discovery shows that ancient microbes lived and thrived in deep, dark parts of the ocean much earlier than scientists thought. It challenges the idea that such microbes needed sunlight and suggests that many similar fossils might have been missed or misunderstood. This changes how we think about early life on Earth and the environments where it could exist.

**What's next step** – Researchers will likely re-examine other wrinkled rock formations worldwide to see if they also show signs of deep-sea microbial life. This could lead to new discoveries about ancient ecosystems and improve how scientists identify fossils formed by microbes versus physical processes.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Wrinkled rocks found deep in the ancient ocean reveal that microbes thrived without sunlight by using chemicals from underwater landslides, changing our view of early life on Earth.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des roches ridées au Maroc révèlent une vie ancienne dans l'océan profond
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques pensaient que certaines textures ridées dans les roches anciennes se formaient uniquement dans des eaux peu profondes éclairées par le soleil, où les microbes pouvaient vivre grâce à la lumière. Ces motifs étaient considérés comme rares ou absents dans les profondeurs de l'océan.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – En 2016, la géologue Rowan Martindale a découvert au Maroc des roches présentant une texture ridée ressemblant à des tapis microbiennes fossilisés. Ces roches se sont formées à près de 180 mètres sous la surface de l'océan il y a plus de 180 millions d'années. Elle et son équipe ont montré que ces microbes profonds n'utilisaient pas la lumière, mais des produits chimiques apportés par des glissements sous-marins pour survivre et créer ces motifs.

**Impact** – Cette découverte prouve que des microbes anciens ont vécu et prospéré dans des zones profondes et sombres de l'océan bien plus tôt que ce qu'on pensait. Elle remet en question l'idée que ces microbes avaient besoin de la lumière et suggère que de nombreux fossiles similaires ont peut-être été négligés ou mal compris. Cela change notre vision de la vie ancienne sur Terre.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs vont probablement réexaminer d'autres formations rocheuses ridées dans le monde pour vérifier si elles montrent aussi des signes de vie microbienne profonde. Cela pourrait mener à de nouvelles découvertes sur les écosystèmes anciens et améliorer l'identification des fossiles.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Des roches ridées découvertes dans l'océan ancien profond montrent que des microbes vivaient sans lumière en utilisant des produits chimiques, changeant notre compréhension de la vie ancienne sur Terre.
articles.personality_title_es: Rocas arrugadas en Marruecos revelan vida antigua en el océano profundo
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos creían que ciertos patrones arrugados en rocas antiguas solo se formaban en aguas poco profundas y soleadas, donde los microbios podían vivir gracias a la luz solar. Se pensaba que estos patrones eran raros o inexistentes en las profundidades del océano.

**Qué pasó** – En 2016, la geóloga Rowan Martindale encontró en Marruecos texturas arrugadas en rocas que parecían tapetes microbianos fósiles. Estas rocas se formaron a casi 180 metros bajo la superficie del océano hace más de 180 millones de años. Martindale y su equipo descubrieron que, en lugar de usar la luz solar, los microbios del océano profundo probablemente usaban químicos de deslizamientos submarinos para sobrevivir y crear estos patrones.

**Impacto** – Este hallazgo muestra que los microbios antiguos vivían y prosperaban en partes profundas y oscuras del océano mucho antes de lo que se pensaba. Desafía la idea de que estos microbios necesitaban luz y sugiere que muchos fósiles similares podrían haber sido pasados por alto o malinterpretados. Esto cambia cómo entendemos la vida temprana en la Tierra.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores probablemente volverán a examinar otras formaciones rocosas arrugadas en todo el mundo para ver si también muestran signos de vida microbiana profunda. Esto podría llevar a nuevos descubrimientos sobre ecosistemas antiguos y mejorar cómo se identifican fósiles formados por microbios.

**Resumen en una frase** – Rocas arrugadas encontradas en el antiguo océano profundo revelan que los microbios prosperaban sin luz usando químicos de deslizamientos submarinos, cambiando nuestra visión de la vida temprana en la Tierra.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed painting of a textured sedimentary rock slab resembling wrinkled elephant skin resting on a softly lit ocean floor at twilight, with abstract patterns symbolizing ancient microbial mats glowing faintly beneath the surface, surrounded by gentle ripples and subtle chemical vents emitting delicate wisps, all rendered in warm, natural earth tones and muted blues.

articles.title: This tiny claw in a 500-million-year-old fossil just rewrote the origin of spiders
articles.summary: What started as routine fossil cleaning turned into a major scientific surprise when researchers uncovered a tiny claw in a 500-million-year-old specimen where no claw should exist. That detail revealed Megachelicerax cousteaui, the oldest known relative of spiders, pushing the origins of this group back by 20 million years. The fossil shows that key features of modern spiders and horseshoe crabs were already emerging during the Cambrian Explosion.
articles.content:
After a long day of teaching, Rudy Lerosey-Aubril turned to a familiar task: preparing a Cambrian arthropod fossil for study. At first glance, the specimen looked typical for its age. But as he carefully removed surrounding material, something unusual appeared. Instead of an antenna, there was a claw.

"Claws are never in that location in a Cambrian arthropod," said Lerosey-Aubril, "It took me a few minutes to realize the obvious, I had just exposed the oldest chelicera ever found."

Oldest Known Chelicerate Identified

In a study published in Nature, Research Scientist Rudy Lerosey-Aubril and Associate Professor Javier Ortega-Hernández, Curator of Invertebrate Paleontology in the Museum of Comparative Zoology - both in the Department of Organismic and Evolutionary Biology at Harvard - describe Megachelicerax cousteaui, a 500 million year old marine predator discovered in Utah's West Desert. It is now recognized as the earliest known chelicerate, a group that includes spiders, scorpions, horseshoe crabs, and sea spiders. This finding extends the known history of chelicerates by about 20 million years.

"This fossil documents the Cambrian origin of chelicerates," noted Lerosey-Aubril, "and shows that the anatomical blueprint of spiders and horseshoe crabs was already emerging 500 million years ago."

Detailed Anatomy of an Ancient Predator

Revealing the fossil's structure required patience and precision. Lerosey-Aubril spent more than 50 hours working under a microscope with a fine needle to expose its features. The animal measured just over 8 centimeters long and preserved a dorsal exoskeleton made up of a head shield and nine body segments.

These two regions had different functions. The head shield carried six pairs of appendages used for feeding and sensing. Beneath the body were plate-like respiratory structures that resemble the book gills seen in modern horseshoe crabs.

The First Clear Evidence of a Chelicera

The most striking feature is the chelicera, a pincer-like appendage that defines chelicerates. This structure separates spiders and their relatives from insects, which instead have antennae at the front of their bodies. Chelicerates rely on grasping appendages, often associated with venom delivery.

Despite the abundance of Cambrian fossils, no clear example of a chelicera from that period had been identified before. This discovery fills that gap and provides direct evidence of when these defining features first appeared.

Bridging a Major Evolutionary Gap

Before this fossil was studied, the oldest known chelicerates came from the Early Ordovician Fezouata Biota of Morocco, dating to about 480 million years ago. The new specimen predates them by 20 million years, placing M. cousteaui near the base of the chelicerate lineage.

It represents a transitional form, linking earlier Cambrian arthropods that seem to lack chelicera with later horseshoe crab-like species known as synziphosurines.

"Megachelicerax shows that chelicera and the division of the body into two functionally specialized regions evolved before the head appendages lost their outer branches and became like the legs of spiders today," explained Ortega-Hernández, "it reconciles several competing hypotheses; in a way, everybody was partly right."

Early Complexity in the Cambrian Explosion

This fossil captures a key moment in the evolution of chelicerates. It shows that important elements of their body plan were already established shortly after the Cambrian Explosion, a time when life was rapidly diversifying.

"This tells us that by the mid-Cambrian, when evolutionary rates were remarkably high, the oceans were already inhabited by arthropods with anatomical complexity rivaling modern forms," Ortega-Hernández added.

Why Early Success Was Delayed

Even with these advanced features, chelicerates did not immediately dominate marine ecosystems. For millions of years, they remained relatively uncommon and were overshadowed by groups such as trilobites. Only later did they expand and eventually move onto land.

"A similar evolutionary pattern has been documented in other animal groups," said Lerosey-Aubril. "This shows that evolutionary success is not only about biological innovation -- timing and environmental context matter."

From Overlooked Fossil to Major Discovery

The fossil was collected from the middle Cambrian Wheeler Formation in Utah's House Range. It was discovered by avocational fossil collector Lloyd Gunther and donated to the Kansas University Biodiversity Institute and Natural History Museum in 1981. For decades, it remained part of a collection of seemingly ordinary specimens until Lerosey-Aubril chose to examine it as part of his research on early arthropods.

Named After Jacques Cousteau

The species name Megachelicerax cousteaui honors French explorer Jacques-Yves Cousteau. Lerosey-Aubril - who is also French - and Ortega-Hernández selected the name to recognize Cousteau's efforts to highlight the beauty and vulnerability of marine life.

"Cousteau and his crew inspired generations to look beneath the surface," said Lerosey-Aubril, "it seemed fitting to name this ancient marine animal after someone who changed the way we see ocean life." Just as Megachelicerax cousteaui has changed how we view chelicerates.

A Group That Still Shapes the Modern World

Today, chelicerates include more than 120,000 species, from spiders and scorpions to mites, horseshoe crabs, and sea spiders. They occupy a wide range of environments on land and in water.

"For thousands of years, these animals have quietly existed among us, deeply influencing our lives from pop-culture to medical and agricultural contributions," Ortega-Hernández concluded. "This fossil discovery sheds new light on their origins."

The Lasting Value of Museum Collections

The researchers also emphasized the importance of scientific collections. Institutions such as the University of Kansas Biodiversity Institute and Natural History Museum preserve specimens for decades, allowing new insights to emerge as scientific understanding evolves. The authors highlighted the work of curators including B. Lieberman and J. Kimmig, whose efforts ensure these collections remain available for future discoveries.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery that pushes back the origin of chelicerates (including spiders) by 20 million years, providing new insights into evolutionary biology and the Cambrian Explosion. This finding has broad significance for understanding the history of life on Earth and showcases the value of museum collections for advancing knowledge.
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists study fossils from the Cambrian period, about 500 million years ago, to understand early life on Earth. Chelicerates are a group of animals that include spiders, scorpions, and horseshoe crabs. Until now, the oldest known chelicerate fossils were about 480 million years old.

**What happened** – While cleaning a fossil found in Utah’s Wheeler Formation, researcher Rudy Lerosey-Aubril discovered a tiny claw called a chelicera where there should have been an antenna. This claw belongs to a new species named Megachelicerax cousteaui. This fossil is now recognized as the oldest known chelicerate, pushing back the origin of this group by 20 million years.

**Impact** – This fossil shows that important body parts of spiders and horseshoe crabs had already developed during the Cambrian Explosion, a time when many new life forms appeared quickly. The claw is a key feature that separates chelicerates from other arthropods like insects. It also links earlier simple animals to more complex ones, filling a gap in how scientists understand evolution.

**What's next step** – Researchers will continue studying fossils like this to learn more about how early animals evolved. The discovery also highlights the importance of museum collections, where old fossils can reveal new information when examined with fresh eyes and new tools.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A tiny claw found in a 500-million-year-old fossil rewrites the timeline of spider relatives and reveals early animal complexity during the Cambrian period.
articles.personality_title_fr: Un fossile de 500 millions d'années révèle le plus ancien parent des araignées
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques étudient des fossiles du Cambrien, il y a environ 500 millions d'années, pour comprendre la vie ancienne sur Terre. Les chélicérés sont un groupe d'animaux qui comprend les araignées, les scorpions et les limules. Jusqu'à présent, les plus anciens fossiles connus de chélicérés dataient d'environ 480 millions d'années.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – En nettoyant un fossile trouvé dans la formation Wheeler en Utah, le chercheur Rudy Lerosey-Aubril a découvert une petite pince appelée chélicère là où il aurait dû y avoir une antenne. Cette pince appartient à une nouvelle espèce nommée Megachelicerax cousteaui. Ce fossile est maintenant reconnu comme le plus ancien chélicéré connu, repoussant l'origine de ce groupe de 20 millions d'années.

**Impact** – Ce fossile montre que des parties importantes du corps des araignées et des limules s'étaient déjà développées pendant l'explosion cambrienne, une période où de nombreuses nouvelles formes de vie sont apparues rapidement. La pince est une caractéristique clé qui distingue les chélicérés des autres arthropodes comme les insectes. Elle relie aussi des animaux plus simples à des formes plus complexes, comblant un vide dans la compréhension de l'évolution.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs continueront à étudier des fossiles similaires pour en apprendre davantage sur l'évolution des premiers animaux. Cette découverte souligne aussi l'importance des collections de musées, où de vieux fossiles peuvent révéler de nouvelles informations grâce à de nouvelles méthodes.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Une petite pince trouvée dans un fossile de 500 millions d'années réécrit l'histoire des parents des araignées et révèle la complexité animale au début du Cambrien.
articles.personality_title_es: Un fósil de 500 millones de años revela al pariente más antiguo de las arañas
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos estudian fósiles del período Cámbrico, hace unos 500 millones de años, para entender la vida antigua en la Tierra. Los quelicerados son un grupo de animales que incluye arañas, escorpiones y cangrejos herradura. Hasta ahora, los fósiles más antiguos conocidos de quelicerados tenían unos 480 millones de años.

**Qué pasó** – Al limpiar un fósil encontrado en la formación Wheeler en Utah, el investigador Rudy Lerosey-Aubril descubrió una pequeña garra llamada quelícero donde debería haber una antena. Esta garra pertenece a una nueva especie llamada Megachelicerax cousteaui. Este fósil es ahora reconocido como el quelicerado más antiguo conocido, adelantando el origen de este grupo en 20 millones de años.

**Impacto** – Este fósil muestra que partes importantes del cuerpo de arañas y cangrejos herradura ya se habían desarrollado durante la Explosión Cámbrica, un momento en que muchas formas de vida aparecieron rápidamente. La garra es una característica clave que separa a los quelicerados de otros artrópodos como los insectos. También conecta animales más simples con otros más complejos, llenando un vacío en la comprensión de la evolución.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores seguirán estudiando fósiles como este para aprender más sobre cómo evolucionaron los primeros animales. El descubrimiento también destaca la importancia de las colecciones de museos, donde fósiles antiguos pueden revelar nueva información con nuevas técnicas.

**Frase clave** – Una pequeña garra encontrada en un fósil de 500 millones de años cambia la historia de los parientes de las arañas y muestra la complejidad animal temprana durante el Cámbrico.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of an ancient, small marine arthropod with a prominent claw-like chelicera emerging from sediment, surrounded by subtle hints of a Cambrian seafloor with simple, muted natural tones, symbolizing the dawn of spider and horseshoe crab ancestors in prehistoric oceans.
